findings.summary: infrastructure/publishing/core.py is a pure re-export shim with no own logic except two thin functions
evidence: Lines 24-47 of publishing/core.py: every import carries '# noqa: F401' because nothing in the module uses those symbols — they exist only to be re-exported through __init__.py
publishing/__init__.py then re-imports everything from .core, creating a two-hop indirection: caller -> __init__ -> core -> citations/metadata/models/platforms
findings.suggestion: Eliminate core.py as an intermediary: move create_publication_package() and create_submission_checklist() directly into the most relevant sibling module (metadata.py or a new package.py), and have __init__.py import directly from citations, metadata, models, and platforms.

findings.summary: infrastructure/llm/review/generator.py imports directly from infrastructure/validation/, creating an unexpected cross-subsystem dependency
evidence: Line 61 of generator.py: 'from infrastructure.validation.pdf_validator import extract_text_from_pdf, PDFValidationError'
The LLM subsystem should receive pre-extracted text from its callers rather than reaching into the validation subsystem
findings.suggestion: Move extract_text_from_pdf out of the call path inside generator.py: callers of the LLM review pipeline should extract text first and pass a string.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/exceptions.py performs OS introspection at exception instantiation time inside __init__
evidence: Lines 422-453 of exceptions.py: MissingDependencyError.__init__ executes 'import platform', 'import shutil', and shutil.which() inside __init__ to generate recovery commands
findings.suggestion: Pre-compute recovery commands lazily or move the OS detection logic to a helper in infrastructure/core/environment.py, passing the result as a parameter to the exception.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/validate_markdown_cli.py re-implements find_markdown_files, collect_symbols, and validate_images locally instead of delegating to the canonical markdown_validator module
evidence: Lines 51-78: find_markdown_files() is implemented from scratch using os.listdir and manual sorting, separate from the infrastructure.validation.markdown_validator.find_markdown_files function
The file comment says 'All business logic is in infrastructure/markdown_validator.py' but then proceeds to implement its own business logic below
findings.suggestion: Remove the local re-implementations and import from infrastructure.validation.markdown_validator.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/doc_scanner.py uses deferred function-level imports for all doc_discovery functions rather than top-level imports, unlike every sibling module in validation/
evidence: Lines 57-92 of doc_scanner.py: each private helper method defers its import inside the method body
audit_orchestrator.py and all other validation sibling modules import at the top of the file normally
findings.suggestion: Move the deferred imports to the module top-level, matching the convention of sibling modules.

findings.summary: Error signaling is inconsistent across infrastructure: some functions return bool/None on failure, others raise custom exceptions, with no clear layer-based rule
evidence: file_operations.py: clean_output_directory() catches OSError and returns False on failure
rendering/core.py: render_all() raises TemplateError on failure
A caller of file_operations.clean_output_directory() must check a bool return value, while a caller of rendering/core.render_all() must handle an exception
findings.suggestion: Establish a clear rule: functions at infrastructure boundaries that encounter unrecoverable failures should raise the appropriate TemplateError subclass. Functions that are expected to partially succeed (batch validators) may return structured results.

findings.summary: Multiple modules catch bare Exception and log without re-raising, silently swallowing errors that callers cannot observe
evidence: integrity.py line 77-79: catches Exception on file hash calculation, logs error, sets dict value to False
rendering/core.py: catches Exception on Beamer slide render failure, then makes a second render attempt inside the except block
findings.suggestion: For batch validators, collect errors into a structured result type rather than converting them to False booleans.

findings.summary: infrastructure/rendering/__init__.get_render_manager() is a one-line wrapper that adds no policy, translation, or behavior over RenderManager(config)
evidence: Lines 22-31 of rendering/__init__.py: get_render_manager(config) does exactly 'return RenderManager(config)'
RenderManager.__init__ already accepts Optional[RenderingConfig] and handles the None case itself
findings.suggestion: Remove get_render_manager() and export RenderManager directly.

findings.summary: The codebase maintains two parallel error-description systems (InfraError constants in errors.py and exception classes in exceptions.py) with no clear rule for when to use each
evidence: errors.py defines InfraError frozen dataclasses used for formatted log messages
exceptions.py defines a full exception hierarchy covering the same domain
pipeline.py imports from both and there is no documented rule distinguishing their use
findings.suggestion: Document the intended division: InfraError constants are for human-readable log output only; exception classes are for control flow.

findings.summary: Two separate modules implement overlapping performance monitoring with different class names, creating a split surface
evidence: infrastructure/core/performance.py defines PerformanceMonitor (a start/stop resource tracker)
infrastructure/core/performance_monitor.py defines CodeProfiler and exports monitor_performance

The __init__.py comment reads 'Named ResourceTracker when disambiguation from performance_monitor.CodeProfiler is needed (both modules expose a class called PerformanceMonitor)', confirming the naming collision is known but unresolved
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into a single infrastructure/core/performance.py module. Eliminate the aliased re-export and the acknowledged naming collision.

findings.summary: One test file uses MagicMock/unittest.mock despite the project's stated no-mocks policy
evidence: Grep for 'MagicMock|mocker.patch|unittest.mock' across tests/ returns tests/infra_tests/llm/test_logging.py as the sole match
The project CLAUDE.md states 'ABSOLUTE REQUIREMENT: Never use MagicMock, mocker.patch, unittest.mock, or any mocking framework'
findings.suggestion: Replace the mock-based assertions in test_logging.py with real HTTP server fixtures (pytest-httpserver is already a dev dependency).

findings.summary: Module-level docstrings across infrastructure consistently open with 'comprehensive' and similar inflated adjectives, a strong AI-generation signal
evidence: Grep for 'comprehensive|sophisticated|robust|seamless|enterprise.grade|production.ready' returns 20+ files, all in infrastructure/
The pattern is uniform across modules from different functional domains, indicating copy-paste docstring generation
findings.suggestion: Replace generic superlative-laden module docstrings with concrete single-sentence descriptions of what the module actually does.

findings.summary: infrastructure/__init__.py catches ImportError on its convenience re-exports and falls through silently, making broken sub-package imports invisible at import time
evidence: infrastructure/__init__.py lines 24-58: a broad try/except ImportError block imports from .core, .documentation, .publishing, .reporting, .validation; on failure it issues warnings.warn with ImportWarning
ImportWarning is filtered by Python by default in non-debug mode, so users running without -Wd will silently get a partially-populated namespace
findings.suggestion: Either remove the try/except and let sub-package import errors surface immediately, or catch and re-raise with a descriptive RuntimeError.

findings.summary: logging_utils.py accumulates 119 importers and owns formatting, progress, timing, decorators, emoji constants, and environment detection — too many unrelated responsibilities for one module
evidence: logging_utils.py re-exports from split modules then adds its own ProjectLogger, log_operation, log_timing, log_function_call, log_success, etc. all in one 700+ line file
The split into logging_formatters/logging_helpers/logging_progress is incomplete: callers import from the hub rather than the focused submodule
findings.suggestion: Give each submodule a stable public path. Reduce logging_utils to a thin re-export shim or remove it in favour of direct submodule imports.

findings.summary: query(), query_short(), query_long(), and query_structured() each independently repeat the same start_time/generation_time/logger.debug timing pattern
evidence: query() at lines 180, 218: start_time = time_module.time() then generation_time = time_module.time() - start_time
query_long() at lines 426, 453: identical pattern duplicated verbatim
query_structured() at lines 500, 545: same pattern a third time
findings.suggestion: Extract a single _timed_generate(prompt, model, options, mode_label) internal method that handles timing and debug logging once.

findings.summary: The three-line pytest environment detection block is copy-pasted verbatim into both setup_logger() and get_logger()
evidence: setup_logger() lines 257-261: is_test_env check with three conditions
get_logger() lines 327-331: identical three-condition expression repeated word-for-word
findings.suggestion: Extract to a module-level _is_test_environment() -> bool function and call it from both sites.

findings.summary: log_header() computes a value and immediately discards it with no effect
evidence: Line 515: EMOJIS['rocket'] if USE_EMOJIS else '' — the expression is evaluated but the result is never assigned or used anywhere in the function body
findings.suggestion: Either assign the result to a variable and use it as a prefix in the log calls, or remove the dead expression entirely.

findings.summary: query_long() manually unpacks options fields into a new GenerationOptions rather than merging/inheriting, making callers' options partially silently dropped
evidence: Lines 440-445: long_options = GenerationOptions(max_tokens=self.config.long_max_tokens, temperature=options.temperature if options else None, ...) — other GenerationOptions fields (top_p, top_k, repeat_penalty, num_ctx) passed by the caller are silently discarded
findings.suggestion: Use dataclasses.replace(options, max_tokens=self.config.long_max_tokens) if options else GenerationOptions(max_tokens=...) so callers' options are merged rather than partially dropped.
